text: The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity through the Palmer's point and places two lateral ports to triangulate the surgical area for optimal access during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies and divides flimsy adhesions within the surgical field, opting to address these easier areas before tackling more complex adhesions.
The surgeon gradually separates the omentum, which is adherent to the bowel, after creating working space by dividing the easier bands and adhesions.
The surgeon encounters the bowel adhered to the abdominal wall, preparing to separate these structures.
The surgeon carefully separates the omentum from the bowel, using traction and counter traction techniques to gradually divide the adherent tissue.
The surgeon uses a 30-degree laparoscopic camera inserted through the Palmer's point port to visualize both sides of the adhesions before proceeding with their division.
The surgeon carefully pulls down the bowel that is adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, gradually creating an entry point.
The surgeon uses a closed grasper to apply traction on the bowel, avoiding direct contact. Sharp dissection is performed to divide the flimsy adhesions, focusing on completing the easier areas first.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction while performing sharp division to separate tissue layers, focusing on the easier areas to increase space before addressing more challenging sections. The flimsy tissue layers are gradually identified and dissected.
The surgeon examines both sides of the adhesion before proceeding to divide the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the incision area at the ileostomy site, where the bowel has herniated through the abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully approaches the hernia site from both sides, gradually drawing down the herniated bowel loop using traction techniques.

The surgeon continues the procedure by alternating between easier and tougher sections, using traction and counter-traction along with sharp division to navigate through the adhesions and separate tissue layers.
The surgeon divides part of the hernia sac, revealing the previously reduced herniated bowel. The peritoneum and a bit of sheath are visible alongside the bowel in this area.
A lupithalon suture is passed through the skin into the abdomen and is used in a continuous fashion to close the hernia defect, ensuring the bowel is not injured during the process.
The surgeon retrieves the lupithalon from the site of entry and ties it outside the abdominal wall, completing the closure of the hernia defect.
The surgeon places a 15x15 cm composite mesh inside the abdominal cavity to cover the hernia defect, completing the final repair step.
The surgeon places upper and lower transfixation sutures to secure the mesh and reinforce the hernia repair site.
The surgeon performs further fixations using multiple 2-0 PDS sutures to secure the composite mesh in place within the hernia repair site.

The surgeon secures the composite mesh in place using approximately fifteen sixteenth sutures, ensuring there are no gaps to prevent bowel herniation between the mesh and abdominal wall. The omentum is then repositioned over the small bowel.
